items.q: If something is legal, is it automatically ethical?
items.correct: No - law sets a minimum standard; actions such as exploiting data in ways users never expected can be lawful yet still widely judged unethical.
items.misconception: Yes - the law defines exactly what is right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Yes - ethics is just the informal name for the legal system.
items.why: Students collapse the two categories. Laws lag behind technology, so many genuine debates - AI bias, persuasive design, surveillance - sit in the gap between legal and ethical.

items.q: Must software be registered before it is protected by copyright?
items.correct: No - in the UK copyright arises automatically when an original work, including source code, is created; no registration is required.
items.misconception: Yes - unregistered software has no copyright protection.
items.random_wrong: Yes - copyright only applies after a patent is granted.
items.why: Patents (which need application) get mixed up with copyright (which is automatic). Code is protected from the moment it is written down.

items.q: Does data protection law prevent organisations from storing any personal data?
items.correct: No - it permits processing personal data when there is a lawful basis such as consent or contract, and it imposes duties like security and accuracy.
items.misconception: Yes - holding personal data about customers is illegal.
items.random_wrong: Yes - only the government may store personal data.
items.why: The law regulates rather than prohibits. The exam-relevant content is the principles and rights it imposes, not a blanket ban that would make commerce impossible.

items.q: Gaining unauthorised access to computer material is an offence under which UK law?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Computer Misuse Act 1990. “Data Protection Act 2018” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Under UK data protection law, individuals have the right to:
items.correct: Access the personal data an organisation holds about them
items.why: Correct answer: Access the personal data an organisation holds about them. “Delete any company's entire database” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Open-source software differs from proprietary software because its:
items.correct: Source code is available to view, modify and redistribute under the licence terms
items.random_wrong: Source code is kept secret by the publisher
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Source code is available to view, modify and redistribute under the licence terms. “Use is always completely unregulated” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is primarily an ETHICAL issue rather than a legal one?
items.correct: Bias in an algorithm used to shortlist job applicants
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bias in an algorithm used to shortlist job applicants. “Hacking into a company server” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Computer Misuse Act passed in the UK?
items.why: Exact answer: 1990. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
